Compartment Exam - 2025

Code: 65/S/1 (set-1)

  1. Please check that this question paper contains 23 printed pages.
  2. Please check that this question paper contains 38 questions.
  3. Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  4. Please write down the serial number of the question in the answer-book at the given place before attempting it.
  5. 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the candidates will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer book during this period.

MATHEMATICS

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:80

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This Question paper contains 38 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. Question paper is divided into FIVE Sections - Section A, B, C, D and E.
  3. In Section A - Question Number 1 to 18 are Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) and Question Number {tex}19 \& 20{/tex} are Assertion-Reason based questions of 1 mark each.
  4. In Section B - Question Number 21 to 25 are Very Short Answer (VSA) type questions, carrying 2 marks each.
  5. In Section C - Question Number 26 to 31 are Short Answer (SA) type questions, carrying 3 marks each.
  6. In Section D - Question Number 32 to 35 are Long Answer (LA) type questions, carrying 5 marks each.
  7. In Section E - Question Number 36 to 38 are case study based questions, carrying 4 marks each.
  8. There is no overall choice. However, an internal choice has been provided in 2 questions in Section - B, 3 questions in Section - C, 2 questions in Section - {tex}\boldsymbol{D}{/tex} and {tex}{2}{/tex} questions in Section - {tex}\boldsymbol{E}{/tex}.
  9. Use of calculator is NOT allowed.

SECTION - A

Questions no. 1 to 18 are multiple choice questions (MCQs) of 1 mark each.
1.

If the matrix {tex}A=\left[a_{i j}\right]_{2 \times 2}{/tex} is such that {tex}a_{i j}=\left\{\begin{array}{l}1, \text { if } i \neq j \\ 0, \text { if } i=j\end{array}\right.{/tex}, then {tex}A+A^{2}{/tex} is equal to:

a. {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex}
b. {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0\end{array}\right]{/tex}
c. {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex}
d. {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0\end{array}\right]{/tex}
1
2.

The domain of {tex}{f}({x})=\cos ^{-1}(2 {x}){/tex} is:

a. {tex}[-1,1]{/tex}
b. {tex}\left[0, \frac{1}{2}\right]{/tex}
c. {tex}[-2,2]{/tex}
d. {tex}\left[-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right]{/tex}
1
3.

The value of the determinant {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc}\cos 75^{\circ} & \sin 75^{\circ} \\ \sin 15^{\circ} & \cos 15^{\circ}\end{array}\right|{/tex} is:

a. 1
b. zero
c. {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
d. {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}
1
4.

For a non-singular matrix X , if {tex}{X}^{2}={I}{/tex}, then {tex}{X}^{-1}{/tex} is equal to:

a. X
b. {tex}{X}^{2}{/tex}
c. I
d. O
1
5.

The cofactor of the element {tex}{a}_{32}{/tex} in the determinant {tex}\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{lll}1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 3 & 1 \\ 3 & 1 & 2\end{array}\right|{/tex} is:

a. {tex}\pm 5{/tex}
b. -5
c. 5
d. 0
1
6.

If A is an identity matrix of order n, then A ({tex}\operatorname{Adj} {A}{/tex}) is a/an:

a. identity matrix
b. row matrix
c. zero matrix
d. skew symmetric matrix
1
7.

If {tex}x=t^{3}{/tex} and {tex}y=t^{2}{/tex}, then {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} at {tex}t=1{/tex} is:

a. {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
b. {tex}-\frac{2}{9}{/tex}
c. {tex}-\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
d. {tex}-\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
1
8.

The area bounded by the parabola {tex}x^{2}=y{/tex} and the line {tex}y=1{/tex} is:

a. {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} sq unit
b. {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} sq unit
c. {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} sq units
d. 2 sq units
1
9.

If the rate of change of volume of a sphere is twice the rate of change of its radius, then the surface area of the sphere is:

a. 1 sq. unit
b. 2 sq. units
c. 3 sq. units
d. 4 sq. units
1
10.

{tex}\int \frac{3 \cos \sqrt{{x}}}{\sqrt{{x}}} {dx}{/tex} is equal to:

a. {tex}-6 \sin \sqrt{{x}}+{C}{/tex}
b. {tex}-6 \cos \sqrt{{x}}+{C}{/tex}
c. {tex}6 \cos \sqrt{{x}}+{C}{/tex}
d. {tex}6 \sin \sqrt{{x}}+{C}{/tex}
1
11.

If {tex}\frac{d}{d x} f(x)=3 x^{2}-\frac{3}{x^{4}}{/tex} such that {tex}f(1)=0{/tex}, then {tex}f(x){/tex} is:

a. {tex}6 {x}+\frac{12}{{x}^{5}}{/tex}
b. {tex}{x}^{4}-\frac{1}{{x}^{3}}+2{/tex}
c. {tex}{x}^{3}+\frac{1}{{x}^{3}}-2{/tex}
d. {tex}{x}^{3}+\frac{1}{{x}^{3}}+2{/tex}
1
12.

In an LPP, corner points of the feasible region determined by the system of linear constraints are {tex}(1,1),(3,0){/tex} and {tex}(0,3){/tex}. If {tex}{Z}={ax}+{by}{/tex}, where {tex}a, b \gt 0{/tex} is to be minimized, the condition on {tex}a{/tex} and {tex}b{/tex}, so that the minimum of Z occurs at (3,0) and (1,1), will be:

a. {tex}a=2 b{/tex}
b. {tex}a=\frac{b}{2}{/tex}
c. {tex}a=3 b{/tex}
d. {tex}a=b{/tex}
1
13.

The maximum value of {tex}Z=3 x+4 y{/tex} subject to the constraints {tex}x+y \leq 1{/tex}, {tex}{x}, {y} \geq 0{/tex} is:

a. 3
b. 4
c. 7
d. 0
1
14.

The general solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=2 x \cdot e^{x^{2}+y}{/tex} is:

a. {tex}e^{x^{2}+y}=C{/tex}
b. {tex}e^{x^{2}}+e^{-y}=C{/tex}
c. {tex}{e}^{{x}^{2}}={e}^{{y}}+{C}{/tex}
d. {tex}e^{x^{2}-y}=C{/tex}
1
15.

If '{tex}m{/tex}' and '{tex}n{/tex}' are the degree and order respectively of the differential equation {tex}1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{3}=\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}, then the value of {tex}(m+n){/tex} is:

a. 4
b. 3
c. 2
d. 5
1
16.

If {tex}|\vec{a}|=1,|\vec{b}|=2{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=2{/tex}, then the value of {tex}|\vec{a}+\vec{b}|{/tex} is:

a. 9
b. 3
c. -3
d. 2
1
17.

Two vectors {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} are such that {tex}|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}{/tex}. The angle between the two vectors is:

a. {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex}
b. {tex}60^{\circ}{/tex}
c. {tex}45^{\circ}{/tex}
d. {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex}
1
18.

A coin is tossed three times. The probability of getting at least two heads is:

a. {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex}
c. {tex}\frac{1}{8}{/tex}
d. {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
1

Questions number 19 and 20 are Assertion and Reason based questions. Two
statements are given, one labelled Assertion (A) and the other labelled Reason
(R). Select the correct answer from the codes (A), (B), (C) and (D) as given below.

  1. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
  2. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
  3. Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.
  4. Assertion (A) is false, but Reason (R) is true. 
19.

Consider the function f : R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R defined as f(x) = x3
Assertion (A): f(x) is a one-one function.
Reason (R): f(x) is a one-one function if co-domain = range.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1
20.

Assertion (A): {tex}{f}({x})=[{x}], {x} \in \mathbb{R}{/tex}, the greatest integer function is not differentiable at {tex}x=2{/tex}.
Reason {tex}(R){/tex}: The greatest integer function is not continuous at any integral value.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1

SECTION B

This section comprises very short answer (VSA) type questions of 2 marks each.
21.(i)

Find the principal value of {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)+2 \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}.

 2

OR
(ii)

Prove that:
{tex}\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{{x}}=\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-{x}}{1+{x}}\right), {x} \in[0,1]{/tex}

 2
22.

If {tex}e^{y}(x+1)=1{/tex}, prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-e^{y}{/tex}.

 2
23.

A ladder 13 m long is leaning against the wall. The bottom of the ladder is pulled along the ground away from the wall at the rate of {tex}2 {~m} / {s}{/tex}. How fast is the height on the wall decreasing when the foot of the ladder is 12 m away from the wall?

 2
24.(i)

Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex}, if the points {tex}(-1,-1,2),(2,8, \lambda){/tex} and {tex}(3,11,6){/tex} are collinear.

 2

OR
(ii)

{tex}\vec{{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{{b}}{/tex} are two co-initial vectors forming the adjacent sides of a parallelogram such that {tex}|\vec{a}|=10,|\vec{b}|=2{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=12{/tex}. Find the area of the parallelogram.

 2
25.

Find the angle between the lines
{tex}\vec{r}=(3+2 \lambda) \hat{i}-(2-2 \lambda) \hat{j}+(6+2 \lambda) \hat{k} \text { and }{/tex}
{tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{{j}}-5 \hat{{k}})+\mu(6 \hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}+2 \hat{{k}}){/tex}.

 2

SECTION C

This section comprises short answer (SA) type questions of 3 marks each.
26.

Find the maximum slope of the curve {tex}y=-x^{3}+3 x^{2}+9 x-30{/tex}.

 3
27.(i)

Find:
{tex}\int \sqrt{4 x^{2}-4 x+10} d x{/tex}

 3

OR
(ii)

Evaluate:
{tex}\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex}

 3
28.

Solve the following LPP graphically:
Maximize {tex}{Z}=2 {x}+3 {y}{/tex}
subject to the constraints {tex}x+4 y \leq 8{/tex}
{tex}2 x+3 y \leq 12{/tex}
{tex}3 x+y \leq 9{/tex}
{tex}x \geq 0, y \geq 0{/tex}

 3
29.(i)

Find the general solution of the differential equation
{tex}\left(2 x^{2}+y\right) d x=x d y .{/tex}

 3

OR
(ii)

Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}-\frac{{y}}{{x}}+\operatorname{cosec}\left(\frac{{y}}{{x}}\right)=0{/tex}; given that {tex}{y}=0{/tex}, when {tex}{x}=1{/tex}.

 3
30.

If {tex}\hat{a}, \hat{b}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{c}{/tex} are unit vectors such that {tex}\hat{a} \cdot \hat{b}=\hat{a} \cdot \hat{c}=0{/tex} and the angle between {tex}\hat{b}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{c}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}, then prove that {tex}\hat{a}= \pm 2(\hat{b} \times \hat{c}){/tex}.

 3
31.(i)

Four students of class XII are given a problem to solve independently. Their chances of solving the problem respectively are {tex}\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex}. Find the probability that at most one of them will solve the problem.

 3

OR
(ii)

The probability distribution of a random variable X is given below:

X 1 2 4 2 k 3 k 5 k
{tex}{P}({X}){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{25}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{25}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{25}{/tex}

Find k, if {tex}{E}({X})=2 \cdot 94{/tex} and also find {tex}{P}({X} \leq 4){/tex}.

 3

SECTION D

This section comprises long answer (LA) type questions of 5 marks each.
32.

If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{rrr}3 & 2 & 1 \\ 4 & -1 & 2 \\ 7 & 3 & -3\end{array}\right]{/tex}, find {tex}A^{-1}{/tex}. Using {tex}A^{-1}{/tex}, solve the given system of equations {tex}3 {x}+4 {y}+7 {z}=14 ; 2 {x}-{y}+3 {z}{/tex} {tex}=4 ; x+2 {y}-3 {z}=0{/tex}.

 5
33.(i)

If {tex}y=\cos x^{2}+\cos ^{2} x+\cos ^{2}\left(x^{2}\right)+\cos \left(x^{x}\right){/tex}, find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}.

 5

OR
(ii)

Find the intervals in which the function given by f(x) = {tex}\frac { 3 } { 10 } x ^ { 4 } - \frac { 4 } { 5 } x ^ { 3 } - 3 x ^ { 2 } + \frac { 36 x } { 5 } + 11{/tex} is

  1. strictly increasing
  2. strictly decreasing
 5
34.

Using integration, find the area of the region
{tex}\left\{(x, y): 0 \leq y \leq x^{2}, 0 \leq y \leq x, 0 \leq x \leq 3\right\} .{/tex}

 5
35.(i)

Find the shortest distance between the lines {tex}l_{1}{/tex} and {tex}l_{2}{/tex} given by:
{tex}l_{1}: \vec{{r}} =\hat{{i}}+2 \hat{{j}}-4 \hat{{k}}+\lambda(4 \hat{{i}}+6 \hat{{j}}+12 \hat{{k}}){/tex} {tex}\text { and } l_{2}: \vec{{r}} =3 \hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}-5 \hat{{k}}+\mu(6 \hat{{i}}+9 \hat{{j}}+18 \hat{{k}}){/tex}

 5

OR
(ii)

Show that the lines {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=z{/tex} intersect. Also, find their point of intersection.

 5

SECTION E

This section comprises 3 case study-based questions of 4 marks each.

Case Study - 1
36.

A window is in the form of a rectangle surmounted by an equilateral triangle on its length. Let the rectangular part have length and breadth x and y metres respectively.
Based on the given information, answer the following questions:

  1. If the perimeter of the window is 12 m, find the relation between x and y. (1)
  2. Using the expression obtained in(i), write an expression for the area of the window as a function of {tex}x{/tex} only. (1)
    1. Find the dimensions of the rectangle that will allow maximum light through the window. (use expression obtained in(ii)) (2)

      OR

    2. If it is given that the area of the window is 50 m2, find an expression for its perimeter in terms of x. (2)
 4

Case Study - 2
37.

During the festival season, there was a mela organized by the Resident Welfare Association at a park, near the society. The main attraction of the mela was a huge swing installed at one corner of the park. The swing is traced to follow the path of a parabola given by {tex}x^{2}=y{/tex}.
Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Let {tex}{f}: {N} \longrightarrow {R}{/tex} is defined by {tex}{f}({x})={x}^{2}{/tex} . What will be the range? (1)
  2. Let {tex}{f}: {N} \longrightarrow {N}{/tex} is defined by {tex}{f}({x})={x}^{2}{/tex} . Check if the function is injective or not. (1)
    1. Let {tex}{f}:\{1,2,3,4 \ldots ..\} \longrightarrow\{1,4,9,16 \ldots .{/tex}{tex}\text {be defined by f(x)} = x^2{/tex}. Prove that the function is bijective. (2)

      OR

    2. Let {tex}f: \mathbb{R} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}{/tex} is defined by {tex}f(x)=x^{2}{/tex} . Show that {tex}f{/tex} is neither injective nor surjective. (2)
 4

Case Study - 3
38.

Two persons are competing for a position on the Managing Committee of an organisation. The probabilities that the first and the second person will be appointed are 0.5 and 0.6 respectively. Also, if the first person gets appointed, then the probability of introducing waste treatment plant is {tex}0. 7{/tex} and the corresponding probability is {tex}0.4{/tex}, if the second person gets appointed.
Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. What is the probability that the waste treatment plant is introduced?
  2. After the selection, if the waste treatment plant is introduced, what is the probability that the first person had introduced it?
 4

{tex}\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{{x}}=\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-{x}}{1+{x}}\right), {x} \in[0,1]{/tex}

Solution:

Put {tex}x=\tan ^{2} \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{x}{/tex}
RHS {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-\tan ^{2} \theta}{1+\tan ^{2} \theta}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1}(\cos 2 \theta){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}(2 \theta){/tex}
{tex}=\theta=\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{x}={/tex} LHS

Solution:

{tex}e^{y}(x+1)=1 \Rightarrow e^{y}=\frac{1}{x+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=-\log (x+1){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{1}{x+1}{/tex}
{tex}=-e^{y} \left[\because \frac{1}{x+1}=e^{y}\right]{/tex}

Solution:

{tex}x^{2}+y^{2}=169{/tex}
Differentiate both sides w.r.t. {tex}t{/tex}
{tex}2 x \frac{d x}{d t}+2 y \frac{d y}{d t}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 12(2)+5\left(\frac{d y}{d t}\right)=0[\because{/tex} when {tex}x=12 m, y=5 m]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d t}=-\frac{24}{5}{/tex}
Hence, the height decreases at the rate of {tex}\frac{24}{5} {~m} / {s}{/tex}

{tex}A(-1,-1,2), B(2,8, \lambda), C(3,11,6){/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=3 \hat{\imath}+9 \hat{\jmath}+(\lambda-2) \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{B C}=\hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+(6-\lambda) \hat{k}{/tex}
Since {tex}A, B{/tex} and {tex}C{/tex} are collinear, {tex}\frac{3}{1}=\frac{9}{3}=\frac{\lambda-2}{6-\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=5{/tex}

Solution:

{tex}|A|=3(-3)-2(-26)+1(19)=62 \neq 0 \Rightarrow A^{-1}{/tex} exists.
cofactor Matrix {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}-3 & 26 & 19 \\ 9 & -16 & 5 \\ 5 & -2 & -11\end{array}\right]{/tex}

Given system of equations can be written as {tex}A^{\prime} \cdot X=B{/tex}
where {tex}X=\left[\begin{array}{l}x \\ y \\ z\end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{c}14 \\ 4 \\ 0\end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, {tex}A^{\prime} . X=B \Rightarrow X=\left(A^{\prime}\right)^{-1} . B{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow X=\left(A^{-1}\right)^{\prime} \cdot B=\frac{1}{62}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}-3 & 26 & 19 \\ 9 & -16 & 5 \\ 5 & -2 & -11\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c}14 \\ 4 \\ 0\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{62}\left[\begin{array}{l}62 \\ 62 \\ 62\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l}1 \\ 1 \\ 1\end{array}\right]{/tex}

Solution:

We have lines
{tex}L_{1}: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}=\lambda{/tex}
and {tex}L_{2}: \frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=z=\mu{/tex}

If lines intersect then there exists a value of {tex}\lambda, \mu{/tex} for which {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3)=(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda+1=5 \mu+4,3 \lambda+2=2 \mu+1{/tex} and {tex}4 \lambda+3=\mu{/tex}
Solving first two equations we get {tex}\lambda=-1, \mu=-1{/tex}
These values of {tex}\lambda=-1, \mu=-1{/tex} also satisfy the third equation.
Thus lines intersect.
Also the point of intersections is (-1, -1, -1).

(i) Find the principal value of {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)+2 \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} . Solution: {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)+2 \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=\left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)+2\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right){/tex} {tex}=\pi{/tex} 2 OR (ii) Prove that: {tex}\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{{x}}=\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-{x}}{1+{x}}\right), {x} \in[0,1]{/tex} Solution: Put {tex}x=\tan ^{2} \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{x}{/tex} RHS {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-\tan ^{2} \theta}{1+\tan ^{2} \theta}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1}(\cos 2 \theta){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}(2 \theta){/tex} {tex}=\theta=\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{x}={/tex} LHS 2 22. If {tex}e^{y}(x+1)=1{/tex} , prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-e^{y}{/tex} . Solution: {tex}e^{y}(x+1)=1 \Rightarrow e^{y}=\frac{1}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=-\log (x+1){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{1}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}=-e^{y} \left[\because \frac{1}{x+1}=e^{y}\right]{/tex} 2 23. A ladder 13 m long is leaning against the wall. The bottom of the ladder is pulled along the ground away from the wall at the rate of {tex}2 {~m} / {s}{/tex} . How fast is the height on the wall decreasing when the foot of the ladder is 12 m away from the wall? Solution: {tex}x^{2}+y^{2}=169{/tex} Differentiate both sides w.r.t. {tex}t{/tex} {tex}2 x \frac{d x}{d t}+2 y \frac{d y}{d t}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 12(2)+5\left(\frac{d y}{d t}\right)=0[\because{/tex} when {tex}x=12 m, y=5 m]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d t}=-\frac{24}{5}{/tex} Hence, the height decreases at the rate of {tex}\frac{24}{5} {~m} / {s}{/tex} 2 24. (i) Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} , if the points {tex}(-1,-1,2),(2,8, \lambda){/tex} and {tex}(3,11,6){/tex} are collinear. Solution: {tex}A(-1,-1,2), B(2,8, \lambda), C(3,11,6){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=3 \hat{\imath}+9 \hat{\jmath}+(\lambda-2) \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{B C}=\hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+(6-\lambda) \hat{k}{/tex} Since {tex}A, B{/tex} and {tex}C{/tex} are collinear, {tex}\frac{3}{1}=\frac{9}{3}=\frac{\lambda-2}{6-\lambda}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=5{/tex} 2 OR (ii) {tex}\vec{{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{{b}}{/tex} are two co-initial vectors forming the adjacent sides of a parallelogram such that {tex}|\vec{a}|=10,|\vec{b}|=2{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=12{/tex} . Find the area of the parallelogram. Solution: Let {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} . {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=12 \Rightarrow|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \cos \theta=12{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(10)(2) \cos \theta=12 \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{3}{5}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \sin \theta=\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)^{2}}=\frac{4}{5}{/tex} Now, area of parallelogram {tex}=|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|=|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}=(10)(2)\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)=16{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} area of parallelogram {tex}=16{/tex} 2 25. Find the angle between the lines {tex}\vec{r}=(3+2 \lambda) \hat{i}-(2-2 \lambda) \hat{j}+(6+2 \lambda) \hat{k} \text { and }{/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{{j}}-5 \hat{{k}})+\mu(6 \hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}+2 \hat{{k}}){/tex} . Solution: Given lines are: {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})+\mu(6 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between these two lines. {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{2(6)+2(3)+2(2)}{\sqrt{4+4+4} \sqrt{36+9+4}}=\frac{22}{2 \sqrt{3} \times 7}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{11}{21} \sqrt{3} \Rightarrow \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{11}{21} \sqrt{3}\right){/tex} 2 SECTION C This section comprises short answer (SA) type questions of 3 marks each. 26. Find the maximum slope of the curve {tex}y=-x^{3}+3 x^{2}+9 x-30{/tex} . Solution: {tex}y=-x^{3}+3 x^{2}+9 x-30{/tex} Slope of the curve, {tex}m=\frac{d y}{d x}=-3 x^{2}+6 x+9{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d m}{d x}=-6 x+6{/tex} For maximum/ minimum slope, put {tex}\frac{d m}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=1{/tex} As {tex}\frac{d^{2} m}{d x^{2}}=-6 \lt 0 \therefore m{/tex} is maximum at {tex}x=1{/tex} Maximum slope {tex}=-3(1)^{2}+6(1)+9=12{/tex} 3 27. (i) Find: {tex}\int \sqrt{4 x^{2}-4 x+10} d x{/tex} Solution: {tex}I=\int \sqrt{4 x^{2}-4 x+10} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \sqrt{(2 x-1)^{2}+(3)^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{2 x-1}{2}\right) \sqrt{4 x^2-4 x+10}+\right.{/tex} {tex}\left.\frac{9}{2} \log \left|(2 x-1)+\sqrt{4 x^2-4 x+10}\right|\right]+C{/tex} 3 OR (ii) Evaluate: {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} Solution: {tex}I =\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{x \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} ...(i) {tex}=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x) \sin (\pi-x)}{1+\cos ^{2}(\pi-x)} d x{/tex} {tex}I =\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{(\pi-x) \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} ...(ii) Adding (i) and (ii) {tex}2 I=\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\pi \sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=\frac{\pi}{2} \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\sin x}{1+\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} Put {tex}\cos x=t \Rightarrow-\sin x d x=d t{/tex} {tex}I =-\frac{\pi}{2} \int_{1}^{-1} \frac{d t}{1+t^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\pi\left[\tan ^{-1} t\right]_{0}^{1}=\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}{/tex} 3 28. Solve the following LPP graphically: Maximize {tex}{Z}=2 {x}+3 {y}{/tex} subject to the constraints {tex}x+4 y \leq 8{/tex} {tex}2 x+3 y \leq 12{/tex} {tex}3 x+y \leq 9{/tex} {tex}x \geq 0, y \geq 0{/tex} Solution: Corner Point Value of {tex}Z=2 x+3 y{/tex} {tex}O(0,0){/tex} 0 {tex}A(0,2){/tex} 6 {tex}B\left(\frac{28}{11}, \frac{15}{11}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{101}{11}{/tex} Maximum {tex}C(3,0){/tex} 6 {tex}Z_{\max }=\frac{101}{11}{/tex} when {tex}x=\frac{28}{11}, y=\frac{15}{11}{/tex} 3 29. (i) Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex}\left(2 x^{2}+y\right) d x=x d y .{/tex} Solution: {tex}\left(2 x^{2}+y\right) d x=x d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{1}{x} \cdot y=2 x{/tex} {tex}\text { I.F. }=e^{-\int \frac{1}{x} d x}=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} Solution is given by, {tex}y \cdot\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=\int 2 x \cdot \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}=2 x+C \text { or } y=2 x^{2}+C x{/tex} 3 OR (ii) Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}-\frac{{y}}{{x}}+\operatorname{cosec}\left(\frac{{y}}{{x}}\right)=0{/tex} ; given that {tex}{y}=0{/tex} , when {tex}{x}=1{/tex} . Solution: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\operatorname{cosec} \frac{y}{x}{/tex} Put {tex}{y}={v x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}={v}+{x} \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {v}+{x} \frac{{d v}}{{d x}}={v}-\operatorname{cosec} {v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\int \sin {v} {d} {v}=\int \frac{{d} {x}}{{x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos v=\log |x|+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \frac{y}{x}=\log |x|+c{/tex} {tex}{x}={1}, {y}={0} \Rightarrow {c}={1}{/tex} {tex}\Longrightarrow \cos \frac{y}{x}=\log |x|+1{/tex} 3 30. If {tex}\hat{a}, \hat{b}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{c}{/tex} are unit vectors such that {tex}\hat{a} \cdot \hat{b}=\hat{a} \cdot \hat{c}=0{/tex} and the angle between {tex}\hat{b}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{c}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} , then prove that {tex}\hat{a}= \pm 2(\hat{b} \times \hat{c}){/tex} . Solution: {tex}\hat{a} \cdot \hat{b}=0 \Rightarrow \hat{a} \perp \hat{b}, \hat{a} \cdot \hat{c}=0 \Rightarrow \hat{a} \perp \hat{c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \hat{a}{/tex} is perpendicular to both {tex}\hat{b}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{c} \Rightarrow \hat{a} \|(\hat{b} \times \hat{c}){/tex} Let {tex}\hat{a}=\lambda(\hat{b} \times \hat{c}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\hat{a}|=|\lambda||(\hat{b} \times \hat{c})| \Rightarrow|\hat{a}|=|\lambda||\hat{b}||\hat{c}| \sin \frac{\pi}{6}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\lambda|=2 \Rightarrow \lambda= \pm 2{/tex} {tex}\therefore \hat{a}= \pm 2(\hat{b} \times \hat{c}){/tex} 3 31. (i) Four students of class XII are given a problem to solve independently. Their chances of solving the problem respectively are {tex}\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{3}, \frac{2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex} . Find the probability that at most one of them will solve the problem. Solution: {tex}P(\text { at most one of them will solve the problem }){/tex} {tex}=P(\text { none of them solves the problem }){/tex} {tex}+P(\text { only one of them solves the problem }){/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{4}{5}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{4}{5}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{3}\right.{/tex} {tex}\left.\times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{4}{5}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{4}{5}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{5}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{19}{45}{/tex} 3 OR (ii) The probability distribution of a random variable X is given below: X 1 2 4 2 k 3 k 5 k {tex}{P}({X}){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{5}{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{25}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{25}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{25}{/tex} Find k, if {tex}{E}({X})=2 \cdot 94{/tex} and also find {tex}{P}({X} \leq 4){/tex} . Solution: {tex}E(X)=2.94{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)+2\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)+4\left(\frac{3}{25}\right)+2 k\left(\frac{1}{10}\right){/tex} {tex}+3 k\left(\frac{1}{25}\right)+5 k\left(\frac{1}{25}\right)=2.94{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow k=\frac{1.56}{0.52} \Rightarrow k=3{/tex} Now, {tex}P(X \leq 4)=P(X=1)+P(X=2)+P(X=4){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{5}+\frac{3}{25}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{41}{50}{/tex} 3 SECTION D This section comprises long answer (LA) type questions of 5 marks each. 32.
